mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes A-Class A160/A170 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM668 engine typically costs £1,200–£2,800 supply only depending on variant (60‑95 PS). A replacement OM668 engine costs on average £819 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units costing up to £2,524. Fitting adds £800–£1,200. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes A-Class CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM668 is a durable engine with a typical lifespan of 240,000–250,000 km (approximately 150,000–155,000 miles) when properly maintained. However, it has known weaknesses: turbocharger failure (rarely exceeding 130,000 km), fuel injector issues, oil leaks from the heat exchanger and injection pump, and EGR valve problems.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes A-Class A160/A170 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are turbocharger failure (the defining weakness of the OM668), fuel injector and fuel system problems (rough idle, misfires, stalling), oil leaks from the heat exchanger and injection pump, and EGR valve failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an A-Class CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £1,500–£4,000 A-Class, a £1,200–£2,800 recon is 30‑75% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the turbo has failed or the engine has suffered major internal failure, get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my A-Class's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM668 engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The A160 CDI uses the OM668.941 (60 PS, 1998‑2001) or OM668.940 (75 PS, 2001‑2004). The A170 CDI uses the OM668.940 (90 PS, 1998‑2001) or OM668.942 (95 PS, 2001‑2004).

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an A-Class CDI engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – turbocharger failure, fuel injector problems, oil leaks, EGR valve failure – are repairable. However, if the engine suffers major internal failure (crank, bearings), a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild. The OM668 is a durable engine when properly maintained.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes A-Class A160/A170 CDI Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Not available — all engines are rebuilt or used

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the W168 A-Class CDI pricing. A replacement OM668 engine costs on average £819 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units costing up to £2,524. Reconditioned engine costs typically range from £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an A-Class CDI engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The engine's sandwich‑concept mounting and transverse orientation add complexity. There is little space under the hood, and for servicing the diesel engine must be lowered with a subframe.

cta.label: → Compare A-Class CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, excessive black smoke from the exhaust, whistling or grinding noise from the engine bay, engine warning light, reduced fuel economy, poor acceleration.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored turbo failure can send debris into the engine).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice power loss or unusual noises.

problems.recommendation: The turbocharger is a known weak point on the OM668, with a lifespan that rarely exceeds 130,000 km. The BorgWarner K03 turbocharger can suffer from bearing wear and oil seal failure. Regular oil changes (using the correct 5W-30 or 10W-40 fully synthetic diesel engine oil) are essential to prolong turbo life. If you notice a sudden loss of power or unusual noises from the engine bay, have the turbocharger inspected immediately.

problems.title: Fuel Injector & Fuel System Issues
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, misfires, poor engine performance, increased fuel consumption, hard starting, engine stalling, fuel leaks from the injection pump.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if rough running or stalling occurs.

problems.recommendation: Clogged fuel injectors can lead to poor engine performance and increased fuel consumption. Malfunctioning injectors may cause rough idling and misfires. The Bosch fuel system is generally reliable, but the fuel pressure regulator can fail. If the engine stalls or runs rough, check for injector leak-off or fuel pressure issues. Regular use of quality diesel and occasional fuel system cleaner can help prevent injector issues.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Heat Exchanger & Injection Pump
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the engine bay.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are a common issue on the OM668, particularly through the heat exchanger seals and from the injection pump. If you notice oil spots under your A-Class or a burning oil smell, investigate immediately. These leaks are usually repairable with new seals or gaskets. Regular oil level checks are essential to prevent low oil pressure and engine damage.

problems.title: EGR Valve Failure
problems.symptoms: Reduced power, rough idle, increased fuel consumption, engine warning light, black smoke from exhaust.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms develop.

problems.recommendation: The EGR valve is a known weak point on the OM668. Over time, soot buildup can cause the EGR valve to stick, leading to reduced power and rough running. Regular motorway driving and using high‑quality diesel can help reduce EGR clogging. If the engine warning light appears with EGR‑related codes, have the system diagnosed promptly.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes A-Class CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes A-Class CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (using 5W-30 or 10W-40 fully synthetic diesel oil)
* Turbocharger in good health — no whistling or smoke
* Clean fuel system — no rough idle or hard starting
* A well‑maintained example — the OM668 is durable when properly cared for
avoidIf: * Any sign of turbocharger wear — a known weak point on the OM668
* Any sign of fuel injector issues — rough running or stalling
* Oil leaks from the heat exchanger or injection pump
* Poor service history — the OM668 is intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes A-Class A160 and A170 CDI are powered by the OM668 1.7‑litre turbodiesel — a durable and efficient engine that was ahead of its time with common‑rail direct injection. The engine has a typical lifespan of 150,000‑155,000 miles when properly maintained. However, it has known weaknesses — turbocharger failure (rarely exceeding 130,000 km), fuel injector issues, oil leaks, and EGR valve problems. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained A-Class CDI is a reliable and economical car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare A-Class CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes A-Class CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any smoke or rough running? (injector or glow plug warning)
2. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
3. Turbo Health — Any whistling, lag, or smoke? (turbo warning)
4. Fuel System — Any hard starting, rough idle, or stalling? (injector warning)
5. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the injection pump and heat exchanger
6. EGR System — Any engine warning lights? Any EGR‑related codes?
7. Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
